paper_title: Formalizing Latent Thoughts: Four Axioms of Thought Representation in LLMs

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: We introduce an axiomatic evaluation framework for latent thought representations in LLMs, comprising metrics that are independent of downstream benchmark scores and reveal representational failures that benchmark accuracy masks. Existing evaluations conflate representation quality with model capacity. Therefore, failures cannot be attributed to the representation rather than to the model that processes it. We formalize four functional axioms (Causality, Minimality, Separability, and Stability) and define a quantitative measure for each, computed directly on the representation independently of downstream accuracy. We audit open-weight LLMs across 23 reasoning tasks (e.g., Spatial Reasoning, Factual QA). We find that no candidate satisfies all four axioms simultaneously, that the representations distinguish task type reliably but cannot distinguish between two questions within the same task, and that the representations encode little information beyond what is already present in the input embedding. The failure is consistent across dense, reasoning-distilled, and RL-trained model families, indicating that the gap is structural rather than a property of model size or training procedure.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs) has increasingly moved from discrete Chain-ofThought (CoT) tokens toward continuous latent representations, with growing body of efficiencymotivated work compressing or replacing explicit CoT steps with continuous vectors [66, 27]. Recent work highlights the limitations of discrete tokenization and decoding for reasoning [31] and replaces discrete steps with continuous representations [9, 81], reporting accuracy gains on complex reasoning benchmarks [73]. However, the field evaluates these continuous thought representations almost exclusively through downstream task accuracy [50]. Probes of continuous reasoning tokens find that distinct reasoning paths collapse to single interpretation in early layers while downstream accuracy remains unchanged [57]. Two prior questions therefore remain open. What functional properties constitute valid thought representation, and how can we measure those properties independently of any downstream task? Even when models maintain accurate internal representations, they may fail at downstream tasks [76]. We identify three gaps that impede principled progress on continuous reasoning. First, there is no principled definition of what thought representation must do, because existing methods optimize heuristic proxies (step counts, token budgets, imitation of explicit CoT) without formal statement of the functional requirements. Second, there is no intrinsic evaluation that measures representation quality independently of downstream accuracy [58], and therefore failures cannot be attributed to the representation rather than to the decoder or prompt. Third, and consequently, the status of current methods is unknown. Recent audits find that latent reasoning tokens are often unnecessary for the predictions they are meant to drive [19], and that latent reasoning does not faithfully implement the structured latent-space search that motivates it [16]. Figure 1: Visualizing the axiomatic properties of Functional Thought Representation Our approach. We propose an axiomatic characterization of thought representations in terms of four functional properties (Causality, Minimality, Separability, and Stability), illustrated in Figure 1. The framework describes the function of thought representation rather than its form. It applies equally to vectors, tensors, or sets of vectors. For each axiom, we define quantitative measure evaluated directly on the source LLM without retraining. We use this suite to audit candidate thought representations produced by Soft Thinking with and without Gumbel noise [81, 73] and Latent Thinking [31, 84] across range of thinking budgets, alongside candidates extracted from last-input-token hidden states. We study five open-weight LLMs spanning dense and sparse mixture-of-experts architectures on the 23 tasks of Big Bench Extra Hard (BBEH) [41]. Across the evaluated source LLMs, the intrinsic protocol exposes failure modes that downstream task accuracy does not. The candidates retain coarse task identity but lose the per-question identity that distinguishes one instance of task from another, and the input-prompt embedding is itself competitive with the candidates on every axis the framework measures. Contributions. Each of the three gaps above is addressed by corresponding contribution. Axiomatic formalization of thought representations. We provide definition of thought representations stated in terms of four functional axioms, and prove their logical consistency, independence, and completeness (Appendix B). Intrinsic evaluation protocol. We introduce measure for each axiom. KL substitution error measures Causality, the Minimality Gap measures Minimality, sameand cross-task discriminator accuracy measures Separability, and the Distributional Consistency Score (DCS) measures Stability. Each measure is computable on the source LLM without retraining and is decoupled from downstream task accuracy. Empirical audit of candidate thought representations. Applying the protocol on BBEH exposes fine-grained representational collapse on per-question identity. No evaluated candidate satisfies all four axioms simultaneously, consistent with concurrent reports of silent representational failures in latent reasoning despite unchanged downstream accuracy [58]. The framework provides principled means to develop and audit candidate thought representations as objects of study, evaluated for what an LLM encodes about problem rather than for the downstream reasoning quality of any chain that follows. Beyond evaluation, the four measures provide researchers explicit, quantifiable optimization targets and the diagnostic resolution to attribute downstream accuracy changes to specific representational properties rather than to an aggregate benchmark score. The four measures are one realization of the axioms, which admit alternative quantifications under the same principle, allowing new candidates and measurement designs in future work.

sections.title: 2 Related work
sections.content: Latent representations. Probing studies demonstrate that LLMs build rich internal representations before outputting tokens, with internal states predicting CoT success prior to completion [1, 48], encoding sparse logical features [34], and correlating with task difficulty [35]. These internal signals motivate representations capturing model intent without reliance on explicit outputs. COCONUT [31] and CODI [63] utilize hidden states to represent or compress multiple reasoning paths into continuous vectors. Architectural variants including Tiny Recursive Models (TRMs) [38, 70] and Encode-ThinkDecode (ETD) [43] update latent representations via recursive networks or iteration over reasoningrelevant layers, and related models propagate persistent latent state [45] or inject recurrence through dedicated middle-layer pathway [10]. Similarly, PonderLM-2 [78] and pause-token approaches [29] train models to think via latent pauses or hidden-state interfaces before generation. Continuous reasoning methods. Soft-token research explores continuous concept-spaces [81, 64], but these methods must be carefully constrained to avoid degenerate greedy behavior [73, 9] and remain competitive with textual CoT only on narrow subset of tasks [77]. Layer-wise analyses further suggest that last-layer activations are used directly for next-token prediction, while middepth representations better balance signal preservation and noise compression [65, 62], and key semantic computations often migrate to these layers [71]. Proposed extraction strategies range from vocab-space constrained optimizers [18] to multi-layer readout modules [14]. These works typically conflate thought representation with reasoning performance. In contrast, we isolate representation quality from reasoning steps, avoiding the discretization errors [31] and heuristic approximations [37] inherent in standard decoding algorithms. Additional related work is in Section A.

sections.title: 3 Formalizing thought
sections.content: 3.1 Semantic equivalence and geometric space Notation. Calligraphic letters (X , Y, S, ) denote spaces, capital letters (X, , Z) denote random variables, lowercase letters (x, y, z) denote specific instances, and bold denotes candidate Thought Representation (TR) throughout the paper. To rigorously characterize the properties of thought representation, we first establish tractable criterion for semantic equivalence between output sequences. Let be the space of all possible generated sequences. We posit the existence of semantic mapping function Φ : S, where is semantic manifold. Two sequences y, are defined as semantically equivalent, denoted sem y, if and only if Φ(y) = Φ(y). We further impose geometric structure on equipped with metric dS (, ). Equivalence implies locality. Non-equivalent sequences nevertheless exhibit varying degrees of proximity. For sequences y1, y2, y3, if y1 and y2 share partial semantic overlap (e.g., distinct numerical answers to the same query) whereas y3 is conceptually disjoint, we require dS (Φ(y1), Φ(y2)) < dS (Φ(y1), Φ(y3)). Computationally, we approximate this metric space using high-dimensional embeddings. We utilize the cosine similarity between embedding representations ey, ey Rd as inversely correlated with the semantic metric dS , consistent with evidence that modern learned text embeddings approximate human semantic judgments across wide range of similarity tasks [4]. 3.2 Thought as latent functional state What functional properties must representation satisfy to qualify as thought, and how can each be measured directly on LLMs? We define thought representation not as communicable linguistic artifact (e.g., Chain-of-Thought) but as Functional Thought T, latent state that mediates the transformation from an Input space to the semantic Output space S. Formally, given an input , the model induces probability distribution over the output (Y x). The functional thought is representation intended to capture the sufficient statistics of this distribution. We explicitly exclude interpretability from this definition. Interpretability is an observerdependent property. In contrast, functional thought is constructed to be mathematically optimal in mediating , which may render it opaque to human inspection. 3 Definition 1 (Idealized Thought Representation Mapping). Let denote the input space and denote the semantic output space of model : S. We define thought representation generator as function : , where represents the thought space. The function is constructed such that it induces an equivalence relation on based on the semantic outputs in S. Specifically, for any pair of inputs xi, xj , the mapping satisfies: g(xi) = g(xj) M(xi) = M(xj) (1) This implies that is many-to-one mapping effectively compressing into by preserving distinctness only if the inputs result in semantically distinct outputs in S. Remark. Stochastic extraction methods (e.g., Gumbel noise) fix global random seed, so remains deterministic in practice. The formal construction is in Section B. 3.3 Quantifying the axioms of Functional Thought We propose that robust thought representation must satisfy four axiomatic properties, formalized below via information theory and probability and shown in Figure 1. Table 1 maps each axiom to its formal requirement and to the metric used to quantify it. Through Appendix B, we prove consistency that follows from an idealized one-hot semantic bottleneck construction. Independence is established by four counter-models, each preserving three axioms while violating the fourth. Completeness follows from bijection between and the reachable semantic manifold SM. Running Example Take = Is 13 prime, and why? with output = Yes. 13 is prime because no integer from 2 to 12 divides it. produced by Mθ. Each output is partitioned into reasoning prefix ypre and an answer suffix ysuf . If is valid thought representation derived from ypre, it must functionally substitute ypre within the computational graph of Mθ, so that conditioning on yields predictive distribution over ysuf indistinguishable from conditioning on the explicit tokens ypre. Here ysuf could be the concluding portion of the same output, such as Therefore, 13 is prime. Quantification. Sensitivity to the answer-window length and number of substituted positions is documented in Section D.6. 2. Minimality. thought representation satisfies optimality iff it compresses the input and retains maximum relevance to the output distribution. This aligns with the Information Bottleneck principle, framing that has recently been applied both to characterize how LLM pre-training approaches minimal sufficient compression [15] and to cast chain-of-thought reasoning itself as bottleneck variable between prompt and answer [49]. Let and denote the random variables over inputs and generated outputs respectively, and let I(; ) denote mutual information. An optimal minimizes I(X; T) subject to constraint on I(T; ): min I(X; T) βI(T; ) (3) This characteristic ensures that filters out nuisance variables in (e.g., irrelevant context or noise) that do not contribute to the generation of the high probability semantic output space. 4 Example. Suppose the input contains two unrelated topics, e.g., Hamlet was written by Shakespeare around 1600. Is 13 prime, and why? If the models output addresses only the primality question, the literary detail did not contribute to that output and should not encode it. If the output addressed both topics, should retain both. Minimality penalises encoding content that did not contribute to y. Quantification. The IB Lagrangian is intractable because I(X; T) and I(T; ) depend on unknown distributions. We construct cross-entropy surrogate that preserves the Lagrangians ranking at β = 2. The surrogate combines three cross-entropies, CE(Y T), CE(X ), and CE(X Y, T), and reduces after dropping TR-independent constants to: IB = CE(X Y, T) CE(Y T) larger IB indicates representation that is simultaneously relevant (T predicts with low residual entropy) and minimal (T contributes negligible additional information about beyond what provides). The reduction assumes I(Y ; X) = 0, which holds when is function of alone1. (4) 3. Separability. Separability defines the functional injectivity of the mapping from semantic content to the latent space. Because logical distinctions in high-dimensional representations are encoded in the curvature and flow structure of the underlying manifold rather than in raw point distances [82], we do not adopt fixed geometric distances (e.g., Euclidean margins) and instead rely on functional discriminability. The representation must contain sufficient topological structure to distinguish between semantically nonequivalent output distributions using bounded capacity projection. Given two inputs x1, x2 that induce disjoint high probability semantic spaces S1 S2 = , their corresponding thought representations Tx1 and Tx2 must be resolvable by an optimal semantic projection ϕ : drawn from bounded hypothesis class (a linear projection followed by linear classification head, consistent with the linear representation hypothesis [54]). Using the semantic metric dS defined over the semantic manifold, we require: dS (cid:0)ϕ(Tx1), ϕ(Tx2)(cid:1) > δ for some ϕ (5) Conversely, if distinct inputs lead to semantically convergent outputs, their representations should reside on the same functional manifold, rendering them indistinguishable under ϕ. Separability thus ensures that contains the necessary decision boundaries to be isomorphic to the semantic space S. Example. The same-task setting pairs with = Is 14 prime, and why?, which yields = No. 14 = 2 7. The inputs differ by single token and the answers are semantically opposite, so bounded classifier acting on Tx and Tx must place them on opposite sides of its decision boundary. cross-task setting pairs with medical-domain prompt such as What are the symptoms of Alzheimers?. Its output occupies disjoint semantic region and must remain just as resolvable. Quantification. We instantiate ϕ as learned binary discriminator fdisc(T, ) [0, 1] drawn from H, which scores the alignment between and candidate output sequence . Positives pair with its corresponding generated sequence. Negatives use two strategies, same-task pairing for fine-grained within-task discrimination and cross-task pairing for cross-domain separability. We realize fdisc as trainable linear projection that maps into the embedding space of frozen LLM backbone, followed by trained classification head optimized with binary cross-entropy. Classification accuracy is the metric. Concurrent analyses of soft-thinking representations [57] report an analogous superposition failure in which distinct reasoning paths become indistinguishable. 4. Stability. The representation must be invariant to surface level lexical variations in the output space and robust to sampling stochasticity. Rather than encoding single realization (Y x), should encode the parameters of the semantic distribution (Sx). This implies two conditions: (1) Mode Collapse Resistance: If (Y x) represents uncertainty or confusion, must reflect this entropy, because LLM generation frequently collapses to small subset of high probability modes and fails to mirror the underlying predictive distribution [83]. (2) Lexical Invariance: For any set of high probability sibling outputs {y1, . . . , yk} drawn from the same input that are semantically equivalent (yi sem yj), the induced representations should satisfy Tyi Tyj , property requiring explicit enforcement against latent divergence under paraphrasing [55]. Example. For lexical invariance, two sibling outputs y1 = Yes. 13 is prime because no integer from 2 to 12 divides it. and y2 = Yes, 13 has no divisors other than 1 and itself. are semantically 1The output embeddings used in experiments violate this assumption by construction and are reported as anchor references rather than IB-Lagrangian estimates; see Section D.5 for the full derivation. 5 equivalent and must induce Ty1 Ty2. For mode-collapse resistance, suppose the model is imperfect and outputs Yes in some generations and No in others, so its high-probability outputs do not all agree. Then (Y x) has positive entropy and must reflect both modes rather than encode just one. Quantification. For candidates that produce single representation per input, lexical invariance holds by construction and we probe mode-collapse resistance only. We quantify distributional uncertainty via the semantic entropy Hx of Kuhn et al. [44], computed by binarizing pairwise cosine similarities between output embeddings at threshold τ to form semantic equivalence classes and setting Hx to the Shannon entropy over class sizes. question with Hx = 0 has all outputs in one class, whereas Hx > 0 indicates spread across semantically distinct outputs. To measure whether linearly encodes this property, we adopt the difference-of-means probe of Cencerrado et al. [12] and report the cross-validated AUROC for predicting Hx > 0. The resulting Distributional Consistency Score (DCS) ranges from 0.5 (random baseline) to 1.0 (perfect discrimination). sections.title: 4 Experimental setup
sections.content: Candidates. We analyze (1) the Last Input Token (LIT) from all layers and (2) LIT from the final layer. Hidden states in the last position of the prompt is what the language-model head projects to logits, encoding the models immediate pre-generation context prior to emitting y. Prior work confirms that linear probes on these pre-generation activations recover non-trivial information about the upcoming generation [48]. We evaluate the two variants as reasoning-relevant computation is often concentrated in middle rather than final layers [10]. We additionally evaluate (3) soft tokens with no noise (ST) [81] and with Gumbel noise (STN) [73], and (4) latent thinking (LT) [84] (see Section C.2). For the soft tokens and latent thinking methods, we test varying thinking steps of 1, 16, 32, 64, and 128. We also evaluate exact and pooled output embeddings and the input prompt embedding. Table layout. Columns follow Table 2. The Output Embedding (OE) block holds two upper-bound references derived from , the Exact variant carrying direct semantic knowledge of and the Pooled variant averaging embeddings of possible generations. Neither variant is reference for Minimality or Stability, where output-based encodings carry their own penalties. The Input Embedding (IE) is the prompt embedding, so candidate failing to outperform IE adds no information beyond the prompt. The Random Vector (RV) is an information-free reference point. Output Emb. Exact, Pooled Candidates Baselines Hidden states, Think. methods IE, RV Table 2: Result-table column groups. Variants Group Models. We evaluate on the 23 tasks of BBEH [41] using the original benchmark prompt. We use five open-weight LLMs chosen to cover range of sizes, training procedures, and architectures  (Table 3)  , specifically Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama3.3-70B-Instruct [30], DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen32B [17], Skywork-OR1-32B [32, 33], and GPT-OSS20B [53]. The selection covers dense, sparse-MoE, reasoning-distilled, and RL-trained paradigms. Table 3: Source LLMs covered by the audit. Source LLM Family Paradigm Llama-3.1 8B Dense Dense Llama-3.3 70B DS-R1-Qwen 32B Dense Skywork-OR1 32B Dense GPT-OSS 20B Instruct Instruct Reasoning-distill Native RL Sparse MoE Adjust effort Generation. We assume that encodes the sufficient statistics of (Y x), so the outputs the model assigns high probability to are those consistent with what captures about the input. For each prompt, beam search approximates this high-probability region of (Y x), and the eight returned sequences of up to 8192 tokens form an empirical 6 representative slice on which each axiom is evaluated. Beyond maximizing output probability, beam search guarantees distinct candidate outputs, reduces sampling variance, and exposes an empirical distribution over reasoning paths [24]. Probes. We utilize frozen LlaMA-3.2-1B [30] backbone with trainable projection that maps thought representations into its token-embedding space, and the discriminator adds trained classification head. The frozen backbone serves as shared decoding surface whose learned representations remain compatible with those of independently trained LLMs [59], while the trainable projection learns features specific to each candidate and source model pair. For the Causality measure specifically, the projection is trained on Mθs own output sequences, thus the KL divergence reflects functional substitution rather than generic transferability. Utilizing shared backbone ensures that the computational cost of evaluating the metric remains constant, independent of the LLMs size. Semantic similarity between outputs for equivalence classes of DCS are computed with EmbedNemotron-8B [5], the leading text embedding model on MTEB [51] at the time of writing. Training parameters and auxiliary details are in Sections C, D.1, E.3 and E.4.

The DCS is the AUROC of probe that predicts from the candidate whether questions beam outputs span more than one semantic equivalence class  (Table 7)  . On the four LLMs that produce non-singleton beam clusters on sizeable fraction of questions, the candidates clear the Random Vector baseline by wide margin, the Output Embedding family ranks highest, and the iterative thinking families exhibit decreasing DCS as the step count grows, with the largest decrease for Latent Thinking. The Input Embedding reference matches or exceeds the iterative candidates and is the highest-scoring representation on Llama-70B, reflecting that distributional uncertainty is largely predictable from the question text alone. Pervariant tables across all candidates and step counts, the per-step DCS trajectory, and the projectionand length-control ablations are reported in Sections C.6, D.2, D.4 to D.6 and D.8. 5.2 Per-family analysis Output Embedding as anchor. The Output Embedding family serves as the upper anchor on axes that reward information about the generated continuation, since these candidates are computed from the output directly. On Causality and Separability the Output Embedding is near the maximum achievable score on every source LLM. In the within-task discrimination setting it is the only family exceeding the random baseline by meaningful margin, with the Exact variant above the Pooled variant. The Output Embedding is not meaningful Minimality reference, as its construction violates the chain-rule simplification that the Minimality decomposition assumes. Section D.7 traces this advantage reaching content related to the question encoded along the Output Embedding directions that other candidates sharing its cluster coordinates do not carry. Cross-axis pattern. Figure 2(b) reports the per-axiom score of each family relative to the Input Embedding reference, averaged across LLMs and computed from the family-best variant per (LLM, axis) pair, with Random Vector row as sanity-check anchor. The heatmap recovers the Separability collapse from Section 5.1 and exposes three additional patterns. On within-task Separability, only the Output Embedding clears the random baseline. On Minimality, the iterative thinking families are mostly above the Input Embedding reference whereas Last Input Token is below it, since Last Input Token encodes input-specific information that is not used in the explicit output but is intrinsic to the input. On Stability, the iterative families and Last Input Token are close to the reference, with Soft Thinking with Gumbel noise as the only outlier. The decrease after adding noise is expected and reinforces why noise is critical step for exploration [18, 73]. The Output Embeddings Causality cell is within bootstrap noise of the Input Embedding reference, reflecting substantive empirical observation rather than normalization artifact. Output Embed8 ding and Input Embedding project into regions of the models space that perturb the continuation distribution by comparable amounts, leaving neither dominant on this axis. Taken together, no iterative thinking family achieves the simultaneous strong clustering, wide within-task spread, and high within-question similarity that the framework rewards. The per-family geometric trajectories and the uniform failure pattern across architectures and sizes are reported in Section D.7. 5.3 Joint behavior and synthesis No candidate thought representation consistently exceeds the Input Embedding reference on any of the four axes when results are averaged across LLMs. The per-LLM advantages reported by Tables 4 to 7 for individual candidates do not generalize  (Table 8)  . In addition, the evaluation does not match what benchmark accuracy alone would predict. model can score well on reasoning benchmark while its thought representations fail to discriminate two questions drawn from the same task. The per-task correlation between Separability and downstream accuracy in panel (c) of Figure 2 confirms that this collapse is not explained by task difficulty. The frameworks contribution is measurement protocol that exposes these gaps at the representation level, without retraining the source model and without dependence on downstream benchmark. Takeaway. No candidate beats the Input Embedding reference on every axis across the tested LLMs, and the iterative thinking variants degrade as the step count grows.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: Table 8: Cells beating IE. Candidate Cells/20 Exact Pooled LIT (all, final) ST@1 ST@16128 STN@1 STN@16128 LT@1 LT@16128 16 16 13 35 7 23 7 23 We introduce an axiomatic evaluation framework for candidate thought representations that runs directly on the source LLM without retraining, instantiated by four measures. We apply this methodology across LLMs that span dense, sparse-MoE, reasoning-distilled, and RL-trained paradigms. The protocol exposes representational collapse on per-question identity that downstream task accuracy does not reveal. By focusing our evaluation on the representation rather than the subsequent reasoning process, our approach provides unified foundation. This principled approach readily adapts to new thought representations and axiom measurement designs. Future contributions can propose additional candidate constructions beyond the soft-thinking, latent-thinking, and hidden-state families considered in this work. Implications. The four axioms serve as explicit optimization targets and diagnostic readouts for thought representations. new candidate can be scored on each axiom independently, so any change in downstream reasoning accuracy is attributable to the property responsible rather than to an aggregate benchmark score that obscures the cause. An audit identifies which axiom is the binding constraint on an existing representation before any retraining is undertaken, and competing representations are compared directly on four-metric profile rather than on single accuracy number. This places future thought representation development on explicit, decomposable, and directly comparable properties that researchers can utilize. Limitations. The lexical invariance sub-property of Stability is not measured because every candidate evaluated here produces vectors identical across paraphrases by construction, leaving the sub-property trivial in our setting. measurement protocol awaits candidate constructions that admit non-trivial paraphrase variation. Measurement cost exceeds that of running single accuracy benchmark, as the protocol requires LLM generations and an additional short probe training step. We position the framework as orthogonal to reasoning benchmark evaluation, where the cost of running the protocol once is small relative to the representation quality information it produces. The empirical audit covers the 23 reasoning tasks of BBEH and five open-weight English-language LLMs spanning range of architectures, sizes, and training paradigms, and our conclusions do not necessarily hold for multilingual workloads or for generations outside reasoning. Finally, the candidates audited here are all obtainable from pre-trained LLM without any additional training, and representations satisfying all four axioms may require specifically designed extraction that is trained explicitly to meet those functional requirements. Applying the protocol to such representations is natural direction for future work.

sections.title: A Extended Related Work
sections.content: Surveys and theoretical analyses. growing set of surveys systematically categorises the emerging space of latent and continuous reasoning methods. Chen et al. [13] provide comprehensive taxonomy organising approaches along two axes: token-wise horizontal methods that replace discrete tokens with continuous counterparts, and layer-wise vertical methods that propagate latent state across transformer depth. Zou et al. [85] offer theoretical characterisation of the fundamental exploration execution trade-off between discrete and continuous reasoning, proving that discrete chain-of-thought is forced into high-certainty regime while continuous representations enable exploration at the cost of amplified noise on computational tasks; they introduce the Symbolic Index as scalar measure of decisional certainty that governs this trade-off. Mondorf and Plank [50] survey evaluation practices for reasoning in LLMs, arguing that downstream accuracy conflates reasoning quality with surfacelevel pattern matching. Sui et al. [66] and Feng et al. [27] survey efficient reasoning from the angle of compute cost and length budgets. Together these works catalogue methods and their downstream trade-offs, whereas our framework evaluates the intrinsic representational quality of the intermediate state independently of the decoding strategy or task accuracy. Latent world models, monitoring, and localization. Beyond reasoning benchmarks, parallel line of work demonstrates that LLMs build structured internal models of the world as they process language. Feng et al. [26] show that latent world states can be extracted as structured propositions via propositional probes, providing direct empirical grounding for our Separability axiom: the hidden states must contain linearly decodable information about the semantic state of the world. Du et al. [21] find that human-like object concept representations emerge naturally in multimodal LLMs without explicit supervision, consistent with the view that the internal geometry supports rich semantic structure. Localizing where this structure resides has received substantial attention. Bandarkar et al. [6] show that swapping transformer layer ranges transfers cross-lingual knowledge across models, and Alabi et al. [2] find that adaptation concentrates in early-to-middle adapter layers, both implying semantically meaningful computations are localized at identifiable depths. Sun et al. [67] show that middle layers can be reordered or skipped with minimal accuracy cost, consistent with mid-depth representations sharing structural properties across depth. Ameisen et al. [3] trace computational circuits through residual stream contributions and demonstrate that feature-level computations shift dynamically across layers depending on the prompt, directly motivating why fixed layer selection strategy for thought representation extraction is insufficient and why our framework tests multiple candidate extraction points. Sentence-level and continuous-space representations. separate family of methods pursues meaning representation at the sentence level rather than the token level. Large Concept Models [68] reformulate language modelling as prediction in sentence embedding space rather than over token vocabularies, leveraging SONAR [22] as the shared multilingual sentence encoder. SONAR-LLM [20] extends this further with an autoregressive transformer that reasons in sentence embedding space and decodes back to tokens, operating at granularity coarser than individual tokens but finer than document-level representations. These methods design representations at fixed semantic granularity chosen priori. Our framework is granularity-agnostic and instead asks whether representations produced at any granularity by an existing model satisfy the four axioms, making it applicable to sentence-level methods as diagnostic tool as well as to the token-level iterative candidates we evaluate here.

sections.title: B Formal Analysis of the Axiomatic System
sections.content: In this section, we provide the theoretical justification for the four axiomatic properties defined in Section 3. We use the same notation as the main text. Appendix-specific symbols are introduced where they first appear. We formally establish the logical framework of the set = {Causality, Minimality, Separability, Stability}. 17 B.1 Consistency (Existence) We first analyze the logical consistency of P. In empirical, frozen language models trained via nexttoken prediction, there exists an inherent tension between Causality (which requires to perfectly mimic the lexically-entangled predictive state of Mθ) and Stability/Minimality (which require to discard lexical information). Proposition B.1. The set of properties is logically consistent. There exists an idealized model configuration and representation that satisfies all four axioms simultaneously. Proof. To prove strict logical consistency, we construct an idealized model whose intermediate representations perfectly disentangle semantics from syntax. Let SM be discrete semantic space. Let be constructed such that its forward pass strictly factors through one-hot semantic bottleneck before projecting to vocabulary logits, construction closely related to the discrete latent bottlenecks used in recent latent reasoning architectures [18]. Let be the one-hot activation at this bottleneck. 1. Causality: Because explicitly generates conditioned entirely on this semantic bottleneck, substituting the explicit sequence with yields identical downstream logits, so DKL(PM (Z ) PM (Z T)) = 0, matching the formal causality criterion of Section 3. 2. Minimality: By construction depends on only through T, so forms Markov chain and is sufficient with I(T; ) = I(X; ). For any deterministic sufficient statistic = (X), I(X; ) = H(T ) I(T ; ) = I(X; ), with equality precisely when is bijective function of T. Hence attains the minimum of I(X; ) over all sufficient and saturates the information-bottleneck objective. This is an existence claim about the idealized configuration M. The empirical question of how closely given frozen Mθ approaches this construction is what Section 5 measures. 3. Separability: Distinct semantic intents map to orthogonal one-hot vectors, perfectly resolvable by any linear projection ϕ H. 4. Stability: Semantically equivalent outputs map deterministically to the same one-hot bottleneck, ensuring perfect lexical invariance at the output level. Mode Collapse Resistance holds vacuously in M: by construction, each input induces deterministic semantic class, so (Y x) is point mass with Hx = 0. There is no distributional uncertainty to represent or collapse, and the one-hot encoding faithfully reflects this zero-entropy output distribution. Because this configuration satisfies without contradiction, the system is logically consistent. Remark B.2. For an arbitrary frozen model Mθ with lexical-semantic entanglement, the four axioms define Pareto-optimal frontier rather than strict intersection. In empirical settings acts as an ϵ-optimal approximation that bounds the tradeoff between causal exactness and semantic stability, consistent with prior evidence that intermediate layers balance information compression against signal preservation [65, 62]. We quantify this frontier empirically in Section 5. B.2 Independence (Non-Redundancy) Proposition B.3. The axioms in are logically independent. No property can be derived solely from the conjunction of the others. Proof. We prove independence by constructing theoretical counter-model for each property that satisfies {p} but violates p. Let be an optimal representation satisfying all axioms in an idealized network. Case 1: Violation of Causality. Construct = π(T), where π is fixed, random permutation of the coordinate dimensions of T. Throughout this case the generation head of is held frozen, while the Separability probe ϕ may be re-fit on the new representation. This asymmetry reflects how each axiom is operationalised in Section 3 (probes test latent geometry and are trained per representation, whereas Causality tests substitution into fixed model whose head is never retrained). 18 Satisfies Minimality: permutation is bijection on coordinates, so I(X; T) = I(X; π(T)) = I(X; T) (mutual information is invariant under invertible deterministic transformations), and the information-bottleneck objective is preserved exactly. Satisfies Separability: Permutations are orthogonal transformations and preserve inner products. Since the Separability probe ϕ is trained on whichever representation is presented, it can absorb π1 in its first linear layer at no cost in norm or expressive power, recovering the separability of T. Satisfies Stability: The permutation is deterministic and depends only on coordinate indices, not on the output, so y1 = y2 = π(T y1 ) = π(T ), preserving lexical invariance. Violates Causality: The frozen generation head expects specific semantic features at specific indices. Because the head is not retrained, it cannot absorb π1, so the permuted representation activates wrong indices and is decoded as different (typically incorrect) semantic class. Consequently PM (Z T) diverges from PM (Z ) and the Causality KL spikes despite the geometry being preserved. Case 2: Violation of Minimality. Construct = [T, ξ(X)], where ξ(X) is high-entropy nuisance vector (e.g., hash of the exact character sequence of X) that is independent of the semantic class. We assume the downstream generation head structurally ignores the dimensions of ξ(X) (e.g., via an attention mask that zeroes them out), so that nuisance information is appended without affecting prediction. Satisfies Causality: Since the head ignores ξ(X), the conditional distribution PM (Z T) depends only on the component and matches PM (Z T) = PM (Z ) exactly. Satisfies Separability: linear classifier in can place zero weight on the ξ(X) coordinates and recover the same decision boundary as on T, so the Separability margin δ is preserved. Satisfies Stability: Stability requires lexical invariance among sibling outputs drawn from the same input x. Because ξ is function of the input alone, all sibling outputs yi sem yj generated from the same inherit an identical ξ(x), so their representations agree. Violates Minimality: Independence of ξ(X) from gives I(X; T) = I(X; T) + H(ξ(X) T) = I(X; T) + H(ξ(X)), so I(X; T) exceeds the bottleneck minimum by Θ(H(ξ(X))) bits, which can be made arbitrarily large by lengthening the hash. The representation therefore carries strictly more information about than any sufficient statistic. Case 3: Violation of Separability. Scope of for this case. The Independence argument below analyzes Separability under the trainable component of and treats the frozen LLM in the empirical realization of fdisc (Section C.5) as fixed feature kernel outside the optimized class. This scoping aligns with the linear representation hypothesis [54], under which high-level semantic concepts are linearly decodable from frozen LLM activations. Because empirical fdisc has access to LLM-induced nonlinear features that the theoretical does not, the empirical test is more permissive. An empirical Separability failure implies theoretical violation under H, but the converse does not hold. The construction below therefore establishes Independence at the theoretical level and conservative refinement at the empirical level. Fix two semantic classes {0, 1}. Encode the representations such that class 0 maps to R0 = {(0, 0, 0), (1, 1, 0)} {0, 1}d and class 1 maps to R1 = {(0, 1, 0), (1, 0, 0)}, where 0 is the zero vector in {0, 1}d2. The four vertices are the XOR configuration of the unit square padded into the ambient d-cube. The deep layers of are parameterized to compute the XOR of the first two coordinates before decoding. Satisfies Causality: The deep network applies the parity function before generation, so matches the intermediate distribution the deep head expects and PM (Z T) = PM (Z ). Approximately satisfies Minimality: The representation occupies four configurations for two classes, contributing fixed overhead of one bit above the minimum-sufficient encoding of the binary class label. This is constant, content-independent cost: I(X; T) = I(X; T) + 1 bit, where the extra bit encodes within-class position but carries no additional information about beyond the class label. The violation of strict Minimality is bounded and does not grow with the semantic complexity of X; for the purposes of the independence argument, this ϵ-deviation from strict Minimality is the same approximation acknowledged in the Remark following Section B.1. Satisfies Stability: Within-class pairs satisfy Eij = 1 in the DCS equivalence matrix (Section 3) and the corresponding latent representations differ by Hamming distance at most two. The operational tolerance the Stability axiom permits is therefore satisfied. Violates Separability: The bounded hypothesis class defined in Section 3 consists of linear projection into an embedding space followed by linear head [54]. The composition of two linear maps is itself linear, so every ϕ realises single halfspace decision sign(wT + b) over T. We show no such halfspace separates R0 from R1. Requiring positive decisions on R0 forces > 0 and w1 + w2 + > 0, while requiring negative decisions on R1 forces w1 + < 0 and w2 + < 0. Summing the last two yields w1 + w2 < 2b, which contradicts w1 + w2 + > 0 whenever > 0. The classes are XOR-configured and provably outside the linear hypothesis class. Beyond non-realizability, gradient-based training of any such ϕ on parity targets needs sample complexity scaling as Ω(d2) before any nontrivial correlation emerges [7], so even the relaxed empirical version of Separability fails. No ϕ therefore attains the required margin δ. Case 4: Violation of Stability. Under Definition 1, is deterministic function of alone, which means every beam drawn from the same input shares the same representation and Lexical Invariance is satisfied trivially. To show that Stability is not logically entailed by the remaining three axioms, we therefore work in the natural extended domain where the generator may depend on the generation trajectory: : . This extension captures stochastic extraction methods (e.g., beam-specific random seeds) and allows Lexical Invariance to be nontrivial requirement. The independence argument below shows that even in this richer domain the other three axioms do not force Lexical Invariance. Ty denotes the representation value associated with output in this counter-model; remains the input-determined component from Definition 1, and αlex(y) is the output-dependent augmentation whose role this case isolates. Construct Ty = [T, αlex(y)], where αlex(y) {0, λ} is single massive scalar that flags trivial lexical feature of the output sequence y, for concreteness the presence of trailing space. We assume the downstream generation head structurally drops this augmented index. This assumption isolates Stability cleanly. Causality, Minimality, and Separability are all preserved precisely because the offending coordinate is suppressed at inference. The case is informative for representations consumed by models without such suppression which is exactly the empirical setting we evaluate, where the frozen LLM has no architectural mechanism to know which coordinate carries lexical noise. Under those conditions, even single uncontrolled lexical-flag dimension destroys output-level invariance. Satisfies Causality: The augmented index is dropped prior to prediction, so the causal logits remain identical to those of T. Satisfies Minimality: By construction T, so αlex(Y ) and I(X; T) = I(X; T) exactly. Minimality is preserved without overhead. Satisfies Separability: Any ϕ can place zero weight on the augmented coordinate and recover the same decision boundary as on T, so the Separability margin δ is preserved. Violates Stability: Two semantically equivalent outputs yi sem yj that differ only in whether they end in trailing space acquire αlex(yi) = αlex(yj). Their representations are then separated by Euclidean distance λ, which can be made arbitrarily large, fundamentally destroying the output-level lexical invariance the axiom requires. Therefore, no axiom logically entails another. B.3 Completeness (Sufficiency) Proposition B.4. The set of properties is complete with respect to the definition of Functional Thought Representation. Any representation strictly satisfying establishes well-defined functional isomorphism between the reachable latent space and the reachable semantic manifold SM. Proof. For each semantic class SM, choose high-probability output with Φ(y) = s, and define ψ : SM by ψ(s) = Ty. We verify that ψ is bijection onto . 1. Well-Defined (Stability and Minimality): Fix representative with Φ(y) = to define ψ(s) = Ty. For any other with Φ(y) = s, we need Ty = Ty. If is sibling output drawn from the same input as y, strict Stability (Lexical Invariance) gives Ty = Ty directly. If is drawn from different input, strict Minimality forces to be function of alone, so Ty = Ty again. Hence ψ(s) is well-defined. 2. Injective (Separability): For distinct classes s1 = s2 with disjoint high-probability semantic spaces, Separability supplies some ϕ that resolves ψ(s1) from ψ(s2) with margin δ > 0. In particular ψ(s1) = ψ(s2), so ψ is injective. 3. Surjective (Minimality and the generator definition): The reachable latent is defined as the image g(X ), so every equals g(x) for some input x, which induces semantic class Φ(M(x)) SM. Minimality (in the idealized limit clarified in the Remark below) additionally forces to carry no information about beyond sufficient statistic for the semantic class, because any excess would raise I(X; T) above the bottleneck minimum. Consequently g(x) coincides with Ty whenever Φ(M(x)) = Φ(y), so = ψ(Φ(M(x))) and ψ surjects onto . 4. Functional Equivalence (Causality): Causality upgrades the set-theoretic bijection to functional one. Each = ψ(s) substitutes for the explicit output sequence inside Mθ and induces the same downstream distribution Pθ(Z T) = Pθ(Z ). Because ψ is well-defined, injective, surjective onto , and functionally equivalent to the explicit generative pathway, it realizes bijection = SM. The four axioms therefore fully determine the structural isomorphism, and no further axiom is required. Remark B.5. The term "completeness" here refers to adequacy of the axiom set in the categoricity sense. No additional axiom is required beyond to pin down the functional isomorphism up to relabeling of semantic classes. The bijection is strict in the idealized limit where Stability holds with equality and the Separability margin is unbounded. Under the empirical tolerance of Stability and the finite δ-margin of Separability, the bijection becomes approximate and is quantified in Section 5.

sections.title: C Training Details
sections.content: C.1 LLM Data Generation The generator models, benchmark, beam count, and maximum generation length are defined in Section 4; all models are loaded in their native precision (bfloat16). From each beam we extract hidden states at every decoding step across all layers, and take the last token of the prefill step as the primary thought representation (position 1, decoding step 0, all layers). The per-model layer count is 33, 81, 65, 65, and 25 for Llama-3.1 8B, Llama-3.3 70B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen 32B, Skywork-OR1 32B, and GPT-OSS 20B respectively. C.2 Soft Thinking and Latent Thinking Generation Soft Thinking (No Noise) replaces discrete decoding with the weighted combination of token embeddings = (cid:80) pvev where pv = softmax(z)v and ev is the token embedding [81]. Soft Thinking with Gumbel Noise applies ˆpv exp((zv + ϵv)/τ ) with ϵv Gumbel(0, 1) and temperature τ = 1.0 [73]. Latent Thinking applies recurrent hidden state updates using protocol similar to 21 COCONUT [31], as implemented in Zou et al. [84]. For all iterative methods, we evaluate steps {1, 16, 32, 64, 128}. Determinism under stochastic extraction. The idealized mapping in Definition 1 is defined deterministically. Extraction methods that include stochastic components, such as Gumbel noise in Soft Thinking, fix global random seed across all evaluations, so each input deterministically produces the same T. The Gumbel perturbation is therefore property of the extraction procedure rather than source of representational randomness, and remains well-defined as deterministic function of x. C.3 Causality Evaluation Protocol The causality evaluation does not involve training. Given test problem with = 8 beams {yk}K and corresponding thought representations {Tk}K 1. Prefixsuffix split: Let ysuf denote the last 50 tokens of yk (the answer suffix) and ypre the preceding tokens (the reasoning prefix), so that yk = [ypre, ysuf ]. Beams shorter than 51 tokens are excluded from this evaluation. k=1, we compute: k=1 2. Baseline distribution: Run the evaluation backbone on the explicit token embeddings of ypre to obtain (ysuf ypre) at the ysuf positions via teacher forcing. 3. Intervened distribution: Replace the prefix embeddings with the projected thought representation Tk to obtain (ysuf Tk) at the same positions. The projection is taken from an output-reconstruction projection trained on the source LLMs output sequences via cross-entropy loss (same training splits as the Minimality probe); see Section D.4 for the ablation comparing this choice against the discriminator projection. The evaluation backbone is LLaMA-3.2-1B [30] with its parameters held fixed; the projection learns to map into the backbones embedding space so that the KL measures whether induces the same functional generative effect on ysuf as ypre, rather than raw cross-model transferability. 4. KL divergence: Compute DKL(P (ysuf ypre) (ysuf Tk)) averaged over ysuf positions. Position indices under tiling. Because all candidates are tiled to the same substitution length (128 positions), the absolute position indices seen by ysuf under LLaMA-3.2-1Bs Rotary Position Embeddings are identical across every candidate for given problem. Any KL inflation arising from the difference between the tiled length and the natural prefix length ypre is therefore constant offset shared by every candidate and does not affect their relative ordering. This is performed on the held-out test split (Section E), minus beams excluded by the length gate above. We report the mean KL across all valid beamproblem pairs. C.4 Minimality Probe Architecture and Training Two probes are trained under the same architecture, an output-reconstruction probe estimating CE(Y T) and conditional input probe estimating CE(X Y, T). Both probes tile to 128 positions. The probe maps through parameter-free LayerNorm, learned linear projection (dthought 2048), second LayerNorm, and learned position-independent offset before prepending the result to the token embedding sequence of the target or conditioning text, which is processed by the frozen backbone. Training uses AdamW with cosine learning-rate schedule (lrmax = 5105, 1 epoch, warmup steps = 20), batch size 64, and cross-entropy loss over the tokenized target sequence. C.5 Discriminator Architecture and Training The discriminator fdisc(T, ) applies parameter-free LayerNorm to T, projects it with learned linear map (dthought 2048), and passes the result through second LayerNorm. The input sequence to the frozen backbone is formed by concatenating the token embeddings of , learned separator embedding, the projected thought vectors, and learned CLS token. The hidden state at the CLS position is classified by two-layer head consisting of Linear(2048 1024), LayerNorm, ReLU, Dropout(0.1), and Linear(1024 1). This pattern of training binary classifier on projected latent activations as read-out mechanism for behavioral signals inside transformer models is consistent with recent work on activation read-outs across LLM families [79]. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss with AdamW, learning rate 1 104, batch size 64, and 1 epoch. 22 Hyperparameter provenance. The probe and discriminator configurations reported in this section, including the projection dimensions, the LLaMA-3.2-1B backbone, and the optimizer settings, were selected from small set of variants explored during initial pilots. The values listed above are fixed across every source LLM and every candidate thought representation reported in this paper. No hyperparameter was tuned on the held-out test split. C.6 Stability Sub-Properties and DCS Diagnostics Sub-property coverage. Candidate representations fall into two empirical categories on the lexicalinvariance axis. The Last Input Token, Pooled Output Embedding, Input Embedding, and Random Vector candidates, together with the iterative thinking families (Soft Thinking with and without Gumbel noise, Latent Thinking), produce single vector per question regardless of which beam is drawn, so Ti = Tj for every beam pair (i, j) within the same question and lexical invariance holds by construction for these candidates. Mode-collapse resistance is the only Stability sub-property that admits nontrivial test for them. The Exact Output Embedding is per-beam, with Ti = emb(yi) derived from each beams generated text independently. For DCS it is aggregated per-question. Because Hx is computed from pairwise cosine similarities between these same Nemotron output embeddings, the DCS score for the Exact Output Embedding confirms the alignment of the metric with the output distribution rather than representational property under evaluation. Input-embedding baseline. We report the embedding of the question text as question-difficulty baseline alongside all thought representations. Because Hx is computed in the same embedding space, this input embedding benefits from structural alignment with the label that model-derived representations do not share. Where it matches or exceeds thought representations, distributional uncertainty is largely predictable from the question text alone, which constitutes finding about model capability rather than flaw of the metric (see Section 5). GPT-OSS-20B as an MoE outlier. GPT-OSS-20B yields non-singleton semantic cluster across beams on only 1.0% of questions at τ = 0.9, against 1646% for the four dense source LLMs. The Random Vector baseline drifts above 0.5 on the same model because the positive class is extremely small. The pattern is consistent with Top-K MoE routing acting as re-convergence force on diverging beams, an architectural feature absent in the four dense source LLMs that pulls beam representations back toward each other after they begin to differ. Bespoke generation techniques tailored to MoE architectures may surface divergent semantic outputs but were avoided in this audit to keep the protocol consistent across source LLMs. 3 sweeps Threshold sweep. Figure over {0.70, 0.80, 0.85, 0.90, 0.95} for one representative per thought-representation family across all five LLMs. All other quantities are held fixed. Only the binarization of the cosine similarity matrix used to compute Hx varies. Rankings are stable across the full range. Families that score above the random-vector baseline at the main-text threshold do so at every other threshold, and families that score near the random baseline do so uniformly. equivalence threshold semantic the τ Cencerrado et al. [12] demonstrate that when Hx is linearly decodable from representation via difference-of-means probe, the uncertainty signal is encoded within it. We note that the converse does not necessarily follow, since representation may encode Hx through non-linear structure that linear probe cannot detect and would in that case score near the random baseline despite carrying the relevant information. DCS scores near the random baseline are therefore evidence against linear encoding of distributional uncertainty rather than definitive evidence against encoding in any form. C.7 Reproducibility and Code Release The full evaluation pipeline is publicly available at https://fard-lab.github.io/ formalize-thoughts under the MIT license. The repository is organized as Hydra project with one runnable script per phase and shared configuration tree. Every random seed used in the pipeline is pinned in the corresponding Hydra config so that any individual phase can be re-executed without further parameter passing. Source LLMs are downloaded from public HuggingFace repositories at the identifiers given in Section 4, BBEH is taken from the official Google DeepMind release under the Apache 2.0 license, the LLaMA-3.2-1B backbone shared by every probe and discriminator 23 Figure 3: DCS versus the semantic equivalence threshold τ for one representative per thoughtrepresentation family across all five LLMs. Rankings are stable across the full range shown. is public HuggingFace asset, and the text embedder used is nvidia/llama-embed-nemotron-8b on HuggingFace. Environment. The repository targets Python 3.12 and CUDA 12.6, with dependencies pinned via pyproject.toml and resolved by uv sync. Optional flash-attention support is documented in the README and is not required to reproduce the reported numbers. Hardware. The vast majority of compute was performed on NVIDIA H100 SXM 80 GB GPUs, including all source-LLM data generation and all discriminator training. NVIDIA A100 40 GB GPUs were used for small subset of probe-training and evaluation runs, without changes to the configuration. Compute totals. All numbers below are H100-equivalent GPU-hours and are reported per source LLM. LLM data generation varies substantially across tasks and source LLMs, ranging from roughly 12 to 48 hours per BBEH task depending on output length and model capacity, and averages near 18 hours per task for an aggregate of approximately 400 hours per LLM. Discriminator training comprises 42 runs per LLM (21 candidates each evaluated under same-task and cross-task pairing), with each run taking roughly 3 to 7 hours and an aggregate of approximately 210 hours per LLM. Minimality probe training comprises 42 runs per LLM (21 candidates each producing the CE(Y T) and CE(X Y, T) probes that enter IB), with each run taking roughly 2 to 4 hours and an aggregate of approximately 125 hours per LLM. Causality evaluation runs in roughly 15 to 20 minutes per candidate, for an aggregate near 6 hours per LLM. DCS evaluation runs in roughly 5 minutes total for all five LLMs combined, since it operates only on cached embeddings and difference-of-means probe. Aggregating across the five source LLMs, the experiments reported in this paper consumed approximately 3,700 H100-equivalent GPU-hours. The full research project consumed additional compute beyond this figure for pilot architecture and hyperparameter exploration, whose final settings underwrite the configurations recorded in Sections and C.5.

sections.title: D Additional Analysis
sections.content: D.1 Bootstrap Confidence Intervals All main-table metrics are reported as µ (cid:98)σB, where µ is the cell mean over the held-out test split and (cid:98)σB is the bootstrap standard error obtained by the percentile method with = 10,000 resamples and fixed random seed. The statistical unit of resampling is the problem, with replacement from the test split. We then recompute the cell-level mean on the resampled problems, and take (cid:98)σB as the sample standard deviation of the resampled means. The reported values are therefore 1σ bootstrap standard errors, and 95% interval is recovered as µ 1.96 (cid:98)σB under the normal approximation, or as the [2.5%, 97.5%] percentiles of the bootstrap distribution when that approximation is undesirable. The axioms differ in how each problem contributes observations, and we resample at the problem level in every case so that within-problem beam correlations are preserved. Minimality input component CE(X T): each problem contributes one per-problem crossentropy. Resample the test split; the effective sample size equals the test-split problem count from Section E. 24 Causality KL(P (ysuf ypre) (ysuf T)): cluster bootstrap by problem, with all valid beams of resampled problem kept together. Beams excluded by the 51-token length gate are absent from the resampled mean, and problems whose beams are all excluded are absent entirely; on Llama-3.1-8B this reduces the effective support to 360 problems. The paired advantage RV KL is resampled on the same support with each (T, RV) pair kept glued together (see below). Discriminator-based DCS: the per-problem discriminator-based DCS score, computed over all off-diagonal beam pairs in the within-problem matrix, is single observation per problem. Resample problems, average per-problem score. Paired advantage versus the noise floor (Causality). Because KL is unbounded above, absolute values are difficult to compare across candidate without shared reference. We therefore compute the paired advantage RV KL(T)g = KL(T)g KL(RV)g per problem g, and average over the Causality support defined above. Pairing on the problem cancels per-problem shifts in the KL scale (the dominant source of dispersion in the raw metric), yielding sign-interpretable statistic where < 0 indicates conveys information about ysuf beyond the evaluation backbones projection of noise, whereas 0 indicates no detectable advantage. The paired bootstrap resamples problems with each (KL(T)g, KL(RV)g) pair kept together, so the resulting standard error quantifies the variance of the paired difference rather than that of the two unpaired means. In Table 10, each cell displays the mean KL(ysuf T) with its marginal bootstrap standard error. D.2 Detailed per-axiom results Tables 9 to 11 report the cell-level mean and bootstrap standard error of the Separability, Causality, and Minimality metrics across the populated source LLMs. Figure 4 reports the corresponding Stability AUROC values. Each candidate occupies one column and each source LLM occupies one row. The cell formatting follows the methodology of Section D.1. Bottom row, DCS as function of thinking steps for the iterative thought families. D.3 Distributional Views of Causality Table 10 reduces each candidate to single number per source LLM. We complement that view with two diagnostics that read the same per-problem records bootstrapped in Section D.1, without reweighting or new measurements. Figure 5 pools all per-beam KLs at the 50-token window into one distribution per representation and reports its CDF, with one panel per representation family. curve sitting to the left has smaller per-problem KL on average. steeper curve has tighter per-problem distribution around the mean reported in Table 10. curve that approaches the top slowly has heavy upper tail of high-KL problems that pulls the mean above the per-problem median. With this reading, the anchor panel separates candidates that the mean alone clusters into single rank, since Exact output embedding lands at similar mean as Random vector but carries longer upper tail. Within the thinking families, increasing the step count shifts the entire distribution rather than only its mean, and Soft thinking with Gumbel noise at 32 steps shows particularly heavy upper tail that is invisible at the mean. Figure 6 decomposes the KL dispersion into between-problem and within-problem components. Values close to 1 indicate that the per-problem mean is carried by problem-level differences, supporting the cluster-bootstrap design of Section D.1, while values close to 0 indicate that the dispersion lives across beams within single problem, so the mean averages over heterogeneous within-problem distribution. The Random Vector and Output Embedding anchors show low ICC values, indicating that their per-beam KL dispersion is concentrated within individual problems rather than driven by problem-level differences. The soft-thinking and latent-thinking families show high ICC, so the scalar mean reported in Table 10 is stable per-problem property rather than an average over heterogeneous within-problem noise. D.4 Causality with the Output-Reconstruction Projection The headline Causality results in Table 10 use the projection trained under the language-modelling objective of the Minimality output probe. The Minimality probe learns projection of the same shape as the discriminators, mapping into the embedding space of the frozen 1B model, but optimised to reconstruct rather than to solve binary discrimination task. We document here the ablation that motivated this choice. We test whether the projection objective is the bottleneck for Causality by substituting the discriminator-trained projection in place of the output-reconstruction projection. Each representation is evaluated at its native substitution length without tiling, so the comparison isolates the projection objective from the length effects examined in Section D.6. The substitution path and KL formula are otherwise unchanged, and the random-vector reference column controls for any fixed projection-induced shift. Table 14 reports the comparison on Llama-3.3-70B for the four representations of Section 3. The output-reconstruction projection meaningfully lowers KL for the latent representations that already live on the source-model manifold, with soft thinking dropping by roughly half on the KL scale and last input token by smaller but separable margin. The exact output embedding moves the other way and the random-vector reference is unchanged, confirming the effect is representation-specific rather 27 Figure 5: Per-beam KL CDFs at the 50-token averaging window on Llama-3.3-70B, with one panel per representation family (anchor candidates, soft thinking, soft thinking with Gumbel noise, latent thinking). Within each thinking family every step count is shown. than fixed projection-induced shift. The discriminator-projection numbers are retained in Table 22 for cell-by-cell comparison. Table 14: Causality KL () on Llama-3.3-70B comparing the discriminator-trained projection (Table 22; Disc) against the output-reconstruction projection used in the main text (Table 10; LM). The paired column reports the per-problem mean of KLLM KLDisc with cluster-bootstrap 95% CI; entries with the entire CI on one side of zero are statistically separable from the projection used in the main text.
