D.5 Information-Bottleneck Decomposition for the Minimality Metric The minimality metric IB of Section 3 is the Information Bottleneck Lagrangian at the symmetric weight β = 2, expressed in cross-entropies probe can compute. We give the derivation, identify the constant offset between IB and the absolute Lagrangian, and characterise the conditions under which the surrogate is exact. 28 Figure 6: Intraclass correlation ICC = σ2 between/(σ2 within) of per-beam causality KL on Llama-3.3-70B, with bars per averaging window. Values above 0.5 indicate that the per-problem mean carries most of the dispersion, validating the cluster bootstrap that resamples problems and keeps within-problem beams glued together (Section D.1). between + σ2 Chain-rule decomposition. The general chain rule for mutual information reads I(X; T) + I(Y ; X) = I(T; ) + I(X; ). When is deterministic function of X, the term I(Y ; X) vanishes because has no residual variance once is known, so the chain rule collapses to I(X; T) = I(T; ) + I(X; ). Substituting into the IB Lagrangian L(β) = I(X; T) β I(T; ) gives L(β) = (1 β) I(T; ) + I(X; ). At β = 2 the two coefficients have equal magnitude with opposite signs, recovering the symmetric trade-off L(2) = I(T; ) I(X; ) (6) BarberAgakov surrogates. Mutual information is intractable because H( ) depends on unknown distributions. For any approximate conditional q, Gibbs inequality gives H(A B) CEq(A B), with equality iff matches the true conditional. Training probe to minimise empirical negative log-likelihood on (B, A) pairs therefore yields tight upper bound on H(A B) in the limit of sufficiently expressive probe class. We use this to estimate the two terms of L(2), I(T; ) = H(Y ) H(Y T) H(Y ) CE(Y T), I(X; ) = H(X ) H(X Y, T) CE(X ) CE(X Y, T). (7) (8) The first follows from the standard BarberAgakov lower bound on mutual information [8]. The second is its conditional analogue, where two probes share the input and differ only in whether is appended. Reduction to IB. Substituting the surrogates into L(2) and grouping T-dependent terms, L(2) (cid:2)CE(X Y, T) CE(Y T)(cid:3) + (cid:2)H(Y ) CE(X )(cid:3) = IB + (9) The constant = H(Y ) CE(X ) depends only on the dataset and the unconditional baseline probe, not on the candidate T. Comparisons of IB across representations are therefore comparisons of L(2) shifted by single offset. Bounding direction and ranking preservation. By Gibbs inequality each CE term upper-bounds the corresponding conditional entropy, so each approximation in Equations (7) and (8) yields lower bound on the mutual information term it estimates. In IB, however, the two CE approximations are subtracted from each other. Any systematic bias shared across candidates that is absorbed into the constant cancels in the difference. Within fixed probe class and fixed source LLM, the residual approximation error is candidate-independent, so the ranking of IB across thought representations is preserved. This is why the metric is read within source LLM rather than across LLMs, as varies with the source LLMs output distribution and must not be compared across models. 29 Figure 7: Pearson between per-example causality KL and input length (top) or output length (bottom), in characters, on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Candidates follow the order of Table 10. Random Vector cross-entropy interpretation. The elevated CE(Y T) observed for the Random Vector anchor is not an out-of-distribution artifact. random vector carries no information about , so probe conditioned on pure noise cannot predict the output sequence and cross-entropy rises to the level of an unconditional language model. The probe is asked to optimize signal that does not exist in T, and the resulting high CE confirms that the metric correctly identifies the absence of output-relevant content rather than distributional mismatch. When the chain-rule assumption fails. The reduction relies on I(Y ; X) = 0. The exact output embedding and pooled output embedding candidates are computed directly from the generated continuation, so is function of as well as and I(Y ; X) > 0. For these rows, the substitution into the Lagrangian carries correction term that does not collapse into TR-independent constant, so IB no longer estimates L(2) even up to C. The reported value still has clear empirical reading. For the exact output embedding, contains by construction, so CE(Y T) collapses toward zero and CE(X Y, T) approaches CE(X ). The metric therefore flags this row as trivially sufficient anchor with no residual leakage, which is the correct behavioural diagnosis even though the IB-Lagrangian interpretation no longer applies. D.6 Length Sensitivity of the Causality Metric The causality KL in Table 10 averages over the last 50 tokens of each generated beam, which raises two length-based concerns. First, the metric might track prompt size rather than thought content. Second, longer generation alone could inflate KL for whichever candidate happens to produce it. Figure 7 examines both. The first concern is ruled out outright. No candidate shows meaningful coupling between KL and input length, so the main-text ordering does not reflect task-to-task variation in prompt size. The second concern resolves cleanly. Output length couples with KL only for the Random Vector lower-bound reference, since its content is uninformative by construction and longer generations accumulate more positions that disagree with the explicit prefix and inflate its KL on long beams. The candidates clearly below this reference in Table 10 show near-zero coupling, so their KL is driven by representational fit rather than by how much the source model chose to generate. Output length is therefore not competing explanation for the main-text ranking but property of the lower-bound reference against which the ranking is measured. We further test whether the choice of 50 tokens itself drives the ranking by recomputing the metric across windows of {10, 25, 50, 100, 200} tokens at no extra source-LLM cost, since each beam saves per-window KL during single causality pass through the discriminator. Figure 8 shows the result. The coarse ordering between candidates is stable across windows, with strong candidates sitting near the bottom of the KL axis and weak candidates near the top at every scale, so the ranking in Table 10 Figure 8: Mean causality KL on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct across Z-windows of {10, 25, 50, 100, 200} tokens, with 95% bootstrap CI bands. One panel per TR family, one line per (TR type, think steps) tuple. does not depend on the exact window length. Fine-grained orderings within clusters of similarlyperforming candidates do shift at the longer windows, where the shared context narrows absolute differences to the point that small method-to-method gaps become statistically indistinguishable. At the shortest window the metric averages over very few positions, where per-token stochastic variance dominates and no candidate cleanly separates from the floor. The 50-token window sits between these two extremes, giving the cleanest separation between candidates while still averaging over enough positions to be statistically stable. third length-based concern is the number of substituted vector positions itself, which varies across candidates. Last input token (all layers) substitutes one position per source-model layer, Soft thinking substitutes one position per thinking step, while embedding candidates substitute single position. To isolate the geometric effect of substitution length from any change in information content, we tile each candidate to common number of positions and recompute KL. Figure 9 reports the result on Llama-3.3-70B for the four candidates from Table 10 that admit clean tiling. The Random vector curve rises markedly with substitution length, even though its content is by construction uninformative about the prefix, and the same trend appears for Exact output embedding with smaller dynamic range. Substitution length therefore inflates KL on candidates that carry no instance information, which establishes that absolute KL magnitudes are not commensurable across candidates of different native lengths. The relative ordering between candidates also reorganises once length is matched, with candidates that sat at the strong end of the table at native length sliding toward the weak end once they are tiled out to the length of the longer candidates. The reorganisation does not nullify Table 10, since tiling one-position candidate produces rank-one substitution that is structurally different from natively multi-position candidate, but it does mean that absolute KL gaps in the table reflect mix of representational quality and substitution geometry. This finding fixes the methodological choice that all subsequent quantitative comparisons feed at common substitution length, so that no representation is rewarded or penalised by the metric simply for being natively shorter or longer than another. The causality results of Table 10 and the minimality probes underlying Table 11 both adopt the same tiled length, and varying-length comparisons are reserved for the diagnostic in Figure 9. Figure 9: Mean causality KL on Llama-3.3-70B as the substituted length varies, with 95% clusterbootstrap CI bands. The single-vector candidates (Exact Output Embedding, Random Vector) are repeated times. Soft Thinking (No Noise) uses its native 128-step trajectory truncated to its first vectors at 128, so different points cover different prefixes of the same trajectory rather than tiled copies of one vector. Last Input Token (all layers) has native length 81 on Llama-3.3-70B and is shown at = 81 (no tiling) and at = 128 (the stack is wrapped to fill the substitution window). D.7 Geometric Analysis of Representational Collapse Table 9 reports near-ceiling cross-task discrimination on most candidates and near-random same-task discrimination on almost all of them, which invites the objection that the same-task failure reflects probe capacity rather than property of the representation. We address this objection by measuring the thought-vector geometry directly, with no trained probe in the loop. Each candidate is flattened to single vector per problem (the full Ld hidden-state stack for LIT, the sd prefix for s-step thinking candidates), and all comparisons use cosine similarity. Two quantities, one per discriminator task. k-NN task purity [74, 11] at k=10. For each thought vector, purity is the fraction of its ten nearest neighbours (by cosine similarity) that carry the same task label. The average over all vectors lies in [0, 1], with uniform random neighbour assignment giving 0.042, and value near 1 means the geometry clusters problems by task. Within-task participation ratio [47, 56] PR = (cid:0)(cid:80) , where σi are the singular values of the centred matrix of within-task vectors for given task (so σ2 are the eigenvalues of its sample covariance), averaged over tasks. PR counts how many directions are needed to describe the within-task variance, ranging from 1 when every within-task vector points the same way up to 1 when the vectors spread over as many orthogonal directions as centering allows, where is the number of within-task problems. On this split 20, so the ceiling is 19, and Random Vector reaches PR = 18.5 close to that ceiling. / (cid:80) σ4 σ2 (cid:1)2 Each clustering quantity tracks one discriminator. Purity must clearly exceed the random-neighbour baseline for cross-task discrimination to succeed, since no probe can recover task structure that is absent from the geometry. PR must clearly exceed 1 for same-task discrimination to succeed, since two within-task vectors that span too few directions are too close together for any learned projection to pull apart. The converse is weaker, because Random Vector achieves the maximum PR by construction and so high PR alone cannot certify that the within-task directions encode instance content. Geometry therefore detects the two failure modes (low purity or low PR) without relying on probe behaviour, but cannot certify success. 32 An anisotropy-adjusted similarity scale. We complement the two clustering quantities with third diagnostic, the within-task self-similarity adjusted for anisotropy, following Ethayarajh [23]. Define cos = coswithin-task coscross-task, where the cross-task pair mean serves as the anisotropy baseline inherent to self-attention [28]. Subtracting this baseline isolates the within-task scale beyond what transformer geometry alone produces. Where Ethayarajh [23] apply the adjustment at the token level, we compute it on flattened example-level vectors so that single number summarises each candidate. cos is orthogonal to k-NN purity and PR, since it varies in similarity scale rather than in cluster structure. Figure 10: Each candidate placed on the (PR, k-NN purity) plane, one panel per LLM. Solid lines trace thinking-family trajectories as the step count grows from 1 to 128. Candidates further to the upper right are preferable: higher PR indicates within-task subspace spread across more directions, and higher k-NN purity indicates that nearest neighbours are drawn from the same task. Figure 10 places every candidate on the (PR, purity) plane for each LLM, and the top-right region remains empty in every panel. LIT, Latent Thinking at one step, Input Embedding, and the two Output Embedding candidates occupy the same region of the plane on four of the five LLMs, with moderate PR below the ceiling and high purity; on GPT-OSS-20B both LIT variants drop to lower purity while the Output and Input Embedding candidates stay in their usual region. The geometry therefore admits cross-task discrimination but constrains within-task variance to narrow subspace regardless of architecture or size. Output Embedding candidates land in this same cluster yet still succeed at same-task discrimination in Table 9, because their within-task directions encode instance content that LITs do not. Geometry alone cannot tell the two cases apart, so the analysis rules out failure without certifying success. Same-task discrimination fails because the within-task geometry of latent thought representations is itself collapsed, either too narrow (low PR), too noise-drifted (low purity), or both, and this holds across every LLM tested. Probe capacity therefore cannot explain the failure. Latent Thinking begins at one step in the high-purity region near LIT on every LLM, but adding latent steps cuts purity by roughly half on each LLM and additionally sheds PR on the larger Llama and on both distilled and RL-trained reasoning models. Soft Thinking begins in the opposite low-PR corner and gains PR steadily as depth grows, but cos contracts in step with the gain (from 0.30 at one step to 0.09 at 128 on the Llama models), so the additional dimensions opened up by depth carry less, not more, anisotropy-adjusted within-task signal. Soft Thinking with Gumbel noise traces the same rightward path at consistently lower purity and lands at cos [0.02, 0.05] across all five LLMs, indistinguishable from Random Vector on that axis. No trajectory in any panel enters the high-PR, high-purity, high-cos region occupied by LIT and Output Embedding. The verdict is uniform across architectures and sizes. The distilled and RL-trained reasoning models produce nearly identical panels in Figure 10, with every clustering quantity and similarity scale agreeing to within few percent across all candidates and only minor offsets in the Latent and Soft Thinking trajectories. The geometric verdict matches the main-text tables across LLMs. Every candidate apart from Output Embedding has either low PR or low purity in each panel, and all of them fail same-task discrimination in Table 9. The DCS floor in Table 26 shares the same origin, because cosine similarity over narrow or noise-drifted within-task subspace cannot reproduce richer semantic equivalence matrix at any threshold. The picture is therefore consistent across architectures and sizes. The failure to discriminate within task is property of the geometry that latent computation produces, not an artifact of any single LLM. cos anchors the same picture on the scale axis. Random Vector is at the lower bound near 0, Input Embedding at the upper bound near 0.52, and LIT and Output Embedding occupy range between roughly 0.18 and 0.45. All three diagnostics converge on the same failure pattern, and none depend on trained discriminator. D.8 Probe Capacity Ablation Section D.7 ruled out the most direct version of the weak-probe objection at the level of the geometry itself, with no trained classifier in the loop. We add complementary line of defense within the probe-based metric by varying the same-task discriminators capacity on the largest source model, Llama-3.3-70B, and showing that the chance-level same-task verdict for the latent-thinking and last-input-token candidates is invariant under three architectures of strictly increasing capacity. The Baseline architecture, used throughout the main paper, projects the source hidden state into the input space of the frozen 1B model through single linear layer. The Deep projection replaces the linear with two-layer MLP and LayerNorm. The Deep projection + UF2 architecture additionally unfreezes the last two transformer blocks of the 1B model. Each step strictly enlarges the trainable-parameter budget, and the deepest tier carries roughly an order of magnitude more trainable parameters than the Baseline. Three thought representations are evaluated as anchors. Last Input Token and Soft Thinking at 128 steps are the focus of the same-task failure mode in the main results. Output Embedding (Exact) acts as the positive control, since it is computed from the generated answer itself. If any architecture has chance to descend below the random-guess plateau, it should descend on this representation. Table 15: Probe capacity ablation on Llama-3.3-70B same-task discrimination. Each cell reports test accuracy and the tail-50 mean of the training BCE loss. Columns increase discriminator capacity from single linear projection (Baseline) through deep MLP projection (Deep) to additionally unfreezing the last two transformer blocks of the frozen 1B backbone (Deep + UF2). ln 2 0.693 is the BCE value at uniform 0.5 output and equates to random guess. Baseline ( 19M trainable) Deep ( 44M trainable) Deep + UF2 ( 165M trainable) Thought representation Acc BCE Acc BCE Acc Last Input Token Soft Thinking at 128 steps Output Embedding (Exact) 0.516 0.513 0. 0.685 0.689 0.494 0.500 0.511 0.617 0.693 0.689 0.631 0.501 0.508 0.642 BCE 0.696 0.692 0. Table 15 reports test accuracy alongside the tail-50 mean of the training-loss curve, where ln 2 0.693 is the BCE value at uniform 0.5 output and equates to random-guess accuracy. Output Embedding (Exact) achieves its highest test accuracy at the Baseline tier (0.726), and adding capacity causes overfitting, with test accuracy declining to 0.617 at the Deep tier and 0.642 at Deep+UF2 despite an order-of-magnitude increase in trainable parameters, while BCE worsens toward the random-guess plateau. Last Input Token and Soft Thinking remain at random-guess accuracy at every capacity tier, with no improvement in accuracy or BCE regardless of how many parameters are available. The Baseline architecture was fixed in pilot experiments and applied consistently across all source LLMs and thought representations (see Section C.5). These ablations confirm post-hoc that it avoids the overfitting induced by deeper tiers while retaining the discriminative accuracy of the positive control. Under the larger architectures, the training loss of the candidates carrying no instance-discriminating content decreases transiently during training, but the tail-50 average over held-out steps remains at the plateau, indicating the optimiser learns batch-specific patterns rather than generalisable structure. 34 Figure 11: Within-task discriminator accuracy versus BBEH pass@1 for candidates averaged across Last Input Token, soft-thinking, latent-thinking, and last-hidden-state variants (left) and the Exact Output Embedding anchor (right), across 23 BBEH tasks and five LLMs. The dashed line marks the chance baseline. Spearman ρ and p-values are annotated on each panel. Two independent attacks on the same objection, the geometric one with no probe in the loop and the probe-based one with probe of increasing capacity, converge on the conclusion that the same-task failure is property of the representation and not of the discriminator. D.9 Relationship to Downstream Task Accuracy natural concern is that within-task separability collapses on hard tasks because the models output distribution itself becomes less structured under difficulty, not because the representation is inadequate. If this were true, the collapse would track downstream task accuracy and tasks with lower pass@1 would show lower within-task discriminability across all candidates. Figure 11 tests this directly by plotting per-task within-task discriminator accuracy against BBEH pass@1 across the 23 BBEH tasks and five LLMs. For thought-representation candidates averaged over the Last Input Token, soft-thinking, latent-thinking, and last-hidden-state families, the pooled Spearman correlation with downstream accuracy is ρ = 0.10 (p = 0.31, = 115), and no individual LLM reaches significance. The Exact Output Embedding anchor, which has direct access to the generated continuation, also shows no significant correlation (ρ = 0.14, = 0.13). Both series are consistent with near-zero correlation across the full range of task difficulties. These results support the interpretation in Section 5.1. The within-task separability collapse is property of the thought representations rather than consequence of tasks being difficult. The framework surfaces representational failures that downstream accuracy does not register.

sections.title: E Dataset and Benchmark Details
sections.content: E.1 BBEH Task Coverage We evaluate on all 23 tasks from BBEH [41]. Table 16 lists the tasks and their categories. For each task, we use all available examples from the BBEH dataset, yielding 4,520 unique problems across all 23 tasks. Each problem is expanded to = 8 beams during generation, for 36,160 beamproblem pairs in total. These 4,520 problems are further partitioned for probe and discriminator training, stratified per task: within each BBEH task, problems are randomly shuffled and split into train / validation / test at an 0.8/0.1/0.1 ratio, so every task contributes proportionally to every split (3,616 / 452 / 452 problems in aggregate). Several BBEH tasks build on earlier benchmarks, and we acknowledge the originating work as requested by the BBEH authors. BoardGame QA originates from Kazemi et al. [40]. Causal Understanding draws on Nie et al. [52] and Kıcıman et al. [42]. Dyck Languages and Word Sorting follow the setup of Tyen et al. [69]. Geometric Shapes is based on Kazemi et al. [39], Linguini 35 Table 16: BBEH tasks used in our evaluation. [41], which implements taskspecific regular expression patterns to extract final answers from free-form model outputs. Table 17 reports per-LLM parsing statistics over every generation. Per-beam parsing failure varies by more than an order of magnitude across the LLMs we evaluate, and the fraction of problems with at least one parsed beam follows the same ordering. For Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct nearly every beam emits one of the canonical answer prefixes, whereas the other LLMs leave substantially larger fraction of beams without any of the prefixes the extractor matches against, and the answer cannot be recovered without other methods. Table 17: Answer-parsing statistics across LLMs over all generations. Per-beam failure is the fraction of beams whose response does not contain any of the BBEH answer prefixes recognised by the official extractor of Kazemi et al. [41]. Recoverable problems reports the fraction of problems for which at least one of the = 8 beams parses successfully, the headroom beam-level verifier could realise. per-beam failure recoverable problems LLM Llama-3.1 8B Llama-3.3 70B DS-R1-Qwen 32B Skywork-OR1 32B GPT-OSS 20B 42.9% 4.3% 34.9% 49.6% 58.3% 74.1% 99.0% 68.1% 51.7% 43.7% E.3 Output-Length Statistics Table 18 and Figures 12 and 13 report character-length statistics of the saved generations across the five LLMs we evaluate, computed over the entire dataset. Two patterns emerge. First, GPT-OSS36 20B, Skywork-OR1-32B and DS-R1-Qwen-32B exceed Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.3-70BInstruct at the median by roughly an order of magnitude, and their interquartile bands reach into the ten-thousand-character range. Second, individual tasks shift the entire column by near-constant offset. Hard combinatorial tasks like Web of Lies, Zebra Puzzles, Multistep Arithmetic and Shuffled Objects remain at the top of every column, while short answer-format tasks like NYCC, Linguini, SportQA and SARC Triples remain at the bottom, and each LLMs relative ordering of tasks is preserved across columns. Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct produces the tightest distribution of the five, with both the narrowest IQR and the lowest mean despite comparable median to the 8B checkpoint. The maximum of 818,647 characters for GPT-OSS 20B originates from single pathological example in the Multistep Arithmetic task. The model emits roughly 7,000 characters of valid reasoning, then enters runaway indentation loop in which each successive opening parenthesis is preceded by an increasing number of leading spaces, growing quadratically until the 8,192-token generation cap is reached. Approximately 816,000 of the 818,647 characters are whitespace. All eight beams degenerate identically due to deterministic beam search. P@1 averages correctness over all beams per problem, while P@8 marks problem solved if at least one beam scores correct, and the gap between the two columns quantifies the margin verifier could realise from the existing beam set. Across tasks, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Skywork-OR1-32B and GPT-OSS-20B remain ahead of the other two on P@1 within few percentage points of each other, and DS-R1-Qwen-32B records the largest P@8 gain relative to its own P@1, while GPT-OSS-20B records the smallest such gain. The bottom rows aggregate the per-task numbers two ways. The micro average pools all problems across tasks, while the adjusted harmonic mean uses the BBEH papers aggregation / (cid:80) 1/(ai + 1), which down-weights uneven performance across tasks. Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Skywork-OR1-32B and GPT-OSS-20B were not evaluated under this aggregation in the original BBEH release, so the corresponding columns extend the benchmarks leaderboard to those checkpoints. sections.title: F Broader Impacts
sections.content: The work is diagnostic protocol for representations inside frozen LLMs and produces no new generative capability, no new dataset, and no deployed system. The protocol contributes to better reasoning models and deeper understanding of functional thought representations in LLMs. We do not identify negative societal effects of the contribution.

sections.title: G Earlier Formulations
sections.content: G.1 Discriminator-Based DCS Evaluation Protocol The earlier formulation of DCS reused the same-task discriminator fdisc to score within-question beam pairs. For each test problem with = 8 beams, two variants of the semantic equivalence matrix were constructed. Eemb {0, 1}KK sets Eemb ij = 1 when the cosine similarity between the Nemotron embeddings of yi and yj exceeds τ = 0.9, and 0 otherwise. Eparse {0, 1}KK sets Eparse = 1 when the extracted final answers from yi and yj match exactly using the official BBEH answer extraction logic [41], with beam pairs for which either side has no extractable answer excluded rather than labelled non-equivalent. The functional similarity matrix [0, 1]KK was defined by the symmetric cross score Mij = 1 2 (fdisc(Ti, yj) + fdisc(Tj, yi)), and the score was the inverse mean absolute error between and over off-diagonal pairs, ij DCSdisc(x) = 1 1 K(K 1) (cid:88) (cid:88) (cid:12) (cid:12)Mij Eij (cid:12) (cid:12). j=i (10) This formulation proved uninformative in practice. The discriminator was trained on cross-question pairs and provided no gradient signal for within-question scoring, so fdisc returned values near 0.5 for all same-question beam pairs. This collapsed DCSdisc to the random baseline for every representation family and source LLM (see Table 26). 40 G.2 Causality with the Discriminator-Trained Projection Table 22 reports the causality KL under the discriminator-trained projection of Section C.5. This was the projection used in our first iteration of the causality protocol, before the projection-swap pilot of Section D.4 motivated adopting the output-reconstruction projection in Table 10. The earlier table is retained so that the effect of the projection swap remains visible cell-by-cell against the current results. Without length normalisation and under the discriminator-trained projection, several causality values are not well calibrated. We retain that analysis here for transparency. Two issues motivated the corrected formulation IB adopted in Section 3 and derived in Section D.5. First, CE corresponds to the IB Lagrangian only at the trade-off weight β = 1, where the chain-rule decomposition cancels the sufficiency reward and leaves only the redundancy penalty I(X; ), so the metric is unbalanced before any empirical concern. Second, the input-reconstruction probe CE(X T) saturates uniformly across representations because no probe in our class can reconstruct the exact lexical phrasing of from any latent T, which collapses the gap into function of CE(Y T) alone. The corrected IB replaces CE(X T) with the conditional CE(X Y, T), which estimates the residual mutual information I(X; ) and does not saturate. The components reported below corroborate the saturation diagnosis on every source model. high CE driven by high CE(X T) (input compression) and low CE(Y T) (output retention) would reflect minimal sufficient T, whereas high gap produced by both conditionals drifting upward reflects that has lost information about both and . Every Llama-3.1-8B cell of CE(X T) has CI that overlaps this anchor, so the input component alone does not discriminate among candidates on this model. The output component does separate RV from every other candidate, since the Random Vector CE(Y T) is above the upper CI bound of every non-RV cell. The two anchor candidates make the rest of the table interpretable. The Output Embedding (Exact) achieves the lowest CE(Y T) on Llama-3.1-8B, since the probe is being asked to predict from representation of itself, and the Pooled variant follows behind with the per-beam information collapsed away. The Input Embedding is at the centre of the candidate cloud, since the input prompt alone determines the high-probability output and probe over its embedding can already predict before any latent computation has happened. Any candidate above the Input Embeddings CE(Y T) has drifted away from the input without acquiring additional -relevant content, while any candidate below it has packed in further output-relevant information beyond what the prompt already carries. The same anchor reading carries to the implied gap. Subtracting the two components above on Llama-3.1-8B places the Input Embeddings CE already in the high 0.9 range, so the prompt itself, 41 before any latent computation, already exhibits the minimality profile that thought representation is supposed to provide. Most thinking candidates land at or below that input-alone gap, and only the Output Embedding (Exact) clearly exceeds it. The proxy therefore separates Random Vector from the rest of the field, but it also reports that latent thinking does not produce more compressed, output-relevant summary of the problem than directly embedding the input prompt.
